subject: AP Psychology
theme: Cognitive psychology examines how we encode, store, retrieve, and use information — encompassing memory systems, language, thinking, problem-solving, and intelligence.
core_concepts: Memory involves three processes: encoding (getting information in), storage (retaining it), and retrieval (getting it back out) — failure can occur at any stage.

The three-stage model of memory progresses from sensory memory (brief, large capacity) to short-term/working memory (limited to about 7 items for ~20 seconds) to long-term memory (unlimited capacity, potentially permanent).
Encoding can be improved through elaborative rehearsal (connecting new information to existing knowledge), deep processing, mnemonics, chunking, and the spacing effect.
Long-term memory is divided into explicit (declarative: episodic + semantic) and implicit (procedural, conditioned responses) — these rely on different brain systems.
Retrieval cues (context, mood, state) improve recall; the encoding specificity principle states that cues present at encoding are most effective at retrieval.
Forgetting occurs through encoding failure, storage decay, retrieval failure, interference (proactive and retroactive), and motivated forgetting.
Language acquisition involves stages (babbling, one-word, two-word, telegraphic) and is debated between Chomsky's nativist view (LAD) and Skinner's learning theory.
Problem-solving can be hindered by fixation (mental set, functional fixedness), while heuristics (availability, representativeness) can lead to cognitive biases and errors in judgment.

key_terms.definition: The process of converting information into a form that can be stored in memory.
The process of retaining encoded information in memory over time.
The process of accessing and bringing stored information into conscious awareness.
A brief, large-capacity memory store that holds unprocessed sensory information for a fraction of a second (iconic) to a few seconds (echoic).
Memory store with limited capacity (~7 items) and duration (~20 seconds without rehearsal).
Baddeley's model of short-term memory as an active system for temporarily holding and manipulating information.
The relatively permanent memory system with virtually unlimited capacity for storing information.
Consciously recalled memories of facts (semantic) and personal experiences (episodic); depends on the hippocampus.
Memories for skills, habits, and conditioned responses that do not require conscious recall; involves the cerebellum and basal ganglia.
A type of explicit memory for personally experienced events tied to a specific time and place.
A type of explicit memory for general knowledge and facts not tied to a specific personal experience.
A type of implicit memory for skills and motor behaviors (e.g., riding a bicycle).
Encoding strategy that connects new information to existing knowledge for deeper, more durable storage.
Organizing information into meaningful groups (chunks) to increase the capacity of short-term memory.
The finding that distributed practice over time produces better retention than massed practice (cramming).
The tendency to recall items at the beginning (primacy) and end (recency) of a list better than those in the middle.
Previously learned information interferes with the recall of new information.
Newly learned information interferes with the recall of previously learned information.
Loftus's finding that misleading post-event information can distort memory of the original event.
Attributing a memory to the wrong source (e.g., confusing an imagined event with a real one).
Judging the likelihood of events by how easily examples come to mind, leading to overestimation of vivid or recent events.
Judging the likelihood of something by how well it matches a prototype, ignoring base rates.
The tendency to search for, interpret, and recall information that confirms one's preexisting beliefs.
The inability to see an object as having a function beyond its typical use, hindering creative problem-solving.
Chomsky's proposed innate brain mechanism that enables children to acquire language naturally.
The idea that the structure of a language influences the way its speakers perceive and think about the world.
A step-by-step problem-solving procedure that guarantees a correct solution but can be time-consuming.
A mental shortcut or rule of thumb that allows quick judgments but is prone to errors and biases.
The way an issue or question is presented (framed) influences decisions and judgments.
The tendency to overestimate the accuracy of one's beliefs and judgments.

exam_tip: The AP exam frequently tests interference (proactive vs. retroactive), memory construction (misinformation effect), and heuristics (availability vs. representativeness) — be sure you can distinguish between each pair and apply them to novel scenarios.
